content.full_text:
Farn Polrrrcrl Pnrcrrcrs CovrutssloN
P.O. Box 807 . -1211 J Street . Srcrrmento. CA 95Sll-0807
(916) -12l-.5660 . Fax (916) 322-()886
September 12,2000
John A. Ramirez
Rutan & Tucker LLP
Post Office Box 1950
Costa Mesa, CA 92628-1950
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-00-191
Dear Mr. Ramirez:
This letter is in response to your request, on behalf of Councilmember Deyna Hodges,
for advice regarding provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").r
QUESTIONS
1. May Councilmember Hodges take part in governmental decisions relating to the
Desert Fashion Plazaproject, and/or Wessman's proposed exchange of easements
involving the city's Plaza Theater property?
2. May Councilmember Hodges take part in governmental decisions involving Granite
Construction?
CONCLUSIONS
1. So long as Wessman does not buy its plumbing supplies principally or exclusively
from Ferguson, Councilmember Hodges may take part in govemmental decisions
involving the Desert Fashion Plazaproject and the Plaza Theater property.
2. The Commission can only offer a general response to your second question, since you
do not describe particular decisions before Councilmember Hodges.
' Govern*entCodesections81000-91015. Commissionregulations appearattitle2,sections 18109- 18996,of
the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-00-191
Page2
FACTS
Deyna Hodges is a member of the Pahn Springs City Council. She is an employee of
Ferguson Enterprises, a plumbing supply business formerly known as Familian Tile, which pays
her a salary and also a commission on some - but'not all - of its sales. Wessman Development,
a business which has had and will have many development projects before the city council, has
over the past twelve months purchased approximately $10,000 worth of goods from Ferguson
Enterprises, resulting in approximately $185 in commissions paid to Councilmember Hodges.
Granite Construction is another customer of Ferguson, which has purchased several
thousand dollars worth of plumbing supplies over the past twelve months. Granite typically
makes purchases from Ferguson in the range of $4,000 to $100,000 per year, although
Councilmember Hodges does not receive commissions on sales to Granite.
Wessman will be involved in two projects on which you seek specific advice. The Desert
Fashion Plazais a retail mall located within a redevelopment area. Wessman has approached the
city with a proposal to enter into a joint venture to purchase the Plaza from its current owner.
This proposal would require the city to approve and enter into a Disposition and Development
Agreement with Wessman. Second, Wessman has approached the city with a proposal relating
to the PlazaTheater, which is owned by the oity, and certain adjacent property owned by
Wessman. Wessman wishes to expand its adjacent property, and wants the city to approve an
agreement whereby Wessman and the city exchange certain easements on these properties.
You have not described any particular project planned by Granite, but you anticipate that
its historical services as general contractor on city contracts will continue into the future, and that
Granite will also continue to work on private development projects where the city must approve
development applications, permits, and the like.
ANALYSIS
The Act's conflict of interest rules prohibit a public official from making, participating in
making, or in any way using his or her official position to influence a govemmental decision in
which the official has a "frnancial interest." (Section 87100.) Section 87103 provides that a
public official has a "financial interest" within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect
on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family, or on any of the
economic interests described in the statute as follows:


o
o
a
a
File No. A-00-191
Page 3
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a
direct or indirect investmenf of $1,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation
18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, oficer, partner, trustee, employee, or
holds any position of management (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b));
A public official has an economic interest inreal property inwhich he or she has a direct
or indirect interest of $1,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2);
A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, includingpromised
income, which aggregates to $25-0 or more within 12 months prior to the decision
(Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3);
A public official has an economic interest in any source of Sfts to him or her if the gifts
aggregate to $300 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(e);
Regulation 18703.4)
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income, assets,
or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family-this is known as the
"personal financial effects" rule (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5).
a
Regulation 18700(b) sets forth in detail the factors that must be shown to establish that a
public official has a conflict of interest in a particular decision.3
Councilmember Hodges is undoubtedly apublic fficial govemed by Section 87100.
(Regulations 18700(b)(1), 18701.). Your request for advice presupposes that she will make,
participate in making, or could use her official position to influence the governmental decisions
you describe. (Regulations 18700(bX2), 18702.) Your questions begin at the next point in the
analysis, whether Councilmember Hodges has economic interests involved in these decisions.
We treat separately the two specific Wessman projects, and your more general question on
decisions relating to Granite Construction.
' An indir".t investrnent or interest means any investnent or interest owned by the spouse of an official or by a
member of the official's immediate family, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official's immediate family, or their agents own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a
l0-percent interest or greater. (Section 87103.) "Immediate family" is defined at Section 82029 as an official's
spouse and dependent children.
I Please bear in mind that the Commission is currently engaged in a comprehensive review of its conflict of interest
regulations. You may monitor its progress through the Commission's website, at www.fopc.ca.gov.


File No. A-00-191
Page 4
The Wessman Projects
As you know, we have provided advice to Councilmember Hodges on two prior
occasions, regarding decisions that involved her employer and Wessmat. (Galanle Advice
Letter, No. A-98-137; Galante Advice Letter, No. 4-98-228.) The analysis in the present case is
much the same. Councilmember Hodges has an economic interest in her employer. (Section
87103(d); Regulation 18703.10).) During the past twelve months, she has received commission
income attributable to Wessman. But Wessman will not be treated as a "source of income"
under Section 87103(c), since the amount of income received over the past twelve months is less
than the statutory threshold of $250, and your account of the facts does not indicate that there is
additional "promised" income (i.e., as-yet-unpaid income to which Councilmember Hodges has a
legally enforceable tight; see Regulation 18703.3(a).)
Councilmember Hodges' economic interest, her employer, is not directly involved in
either of the Wessman projects you describe (Regulation 18704.1), and it will therefore be
treated as "indirectly" involved. After determining an economic interest's level of involvement
in a governmental decision, the next analytical step is identiffing the materiality standard
applicable to the economic interest. (Regulation 18700(5).) We understand that Ferguson is a
,'t,rsiness entity" as defined at Section 82005. Regulation 18705.1 provides the materiality
standards for effects on business entities, in a series of monetary thresholds tailored to the
economic size or worth of the business entity. You have not furnished the information required.
to identiff the precise standard applicable to Ferguson, but Councilmember Hodges can readily
make this determination for herself. To illustrate the analysis, we wrll presume thatthe
appropriate materiality standard is the one given by Regulation 18705.1(bX7).
Regulation 18705(bX7) provides that the effect of a decision on a business entity is
material if the decision will result in an increase or decrease in the business's gloss revenues of
$10,000 or more during a fiscal year; or in an increase or decrease in the value of its assets or
liabilities in the amount of $10,000 or more during a fiscal year; or if the decision will result in
the business incurring or avoiding additional expenses, or reducing or eliminating existing
expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more.
Once the appropriate materiality standard has been identified, the next step in the process
is deciding whether it is "reasonably foreseeable" that the decision in question will have a
"material" financial effect on the business entity. @egulation 18700(6).) A financial effect is
"reasonably foreseeable" if it is "substantially likely." @egulation 18706.) The question reduces
down to this: is it substantially likely that, as a result of a given decision, Wessman will purchase
supplies from Ferguson in sufficient quantities that the purchases amount to a material financial
effect? If we assume that the standard of Regulation 18705(b)(7) is appropriate to Ferguson, this
would mean that, if it is reasonably foreseeable that Wessman would purchase $10,000 of
supplies from Ferguson as a result of a particular decision, Councilmember Hodges has a
disqualifying financial interest in that decision.


File No. A-00-191
Page 5
The Commission faced a similar question irln re Thorner (1975),1 FPPC Ops. 198,
its seminal discussion of foreseeability under the Act. In Thorner. the Commission distinguished
between contract bidders who were regular customers of a business in which a public official had
an economic interest, and bidders who also made significant purchases from vendors in which
the official didnot have an economic interest. The Commission concluded that a decision to
award a contract to a bidder who did not buy principally or exclusively from the official's
business was not "substantially likely" to have a material financial effect on that business.
As we understand matters, Wessman is an "occasional" customer of Ferguson, which we
assume to mean that Wessman does not buy its plumbing supplies principally or exclusively
from Ferguson. So long as that is a correct statement of the relationship between these two
businesses, the decisions you have described to us, involving Wessman, will not foreseeably
have a material financial effect on Ferguson. Councilmember Hodges may therefore take part in
these two decisions involving Wessman.
In the past, we have considered a theoretical possibility that Councilmember Hodges'
govenrmental decisions might cause her employer to alter the terms or conditions of her
employment, whether positively or negatively, thereby creating a "personal financial effect.'{
You have repeatedly indicated that the owner of Ferguson has no intention of altering the
employment relationship in response to Councilmember Hodges' official actions, and there has
apparently been no such effect over the yeaxs. It is therefore appropriate to base our conflicts
analysis on the foreseeability of material financial effect on Ferguson, not on the more remote
possibility that an effect on Ferguson might generate a secondary, personal financial effect on
Councilmember Hodges.
Decisions Involving Granite Construction
Because you have not described any particular decisions involving Granite Construction,
we can only provide you with general advice on the analysis of potential conflicts of interest in
decisions involving this firm, which we assume to be a "business entity" within the meaning of
Section 82005. Thus when a govemmental decision involving Granite comes before
Councilmember Hodges, she must determine whether her employer is directly or indirectly
involved in the decision and identiff the materiality standard appropriate to her employer, as
discussed in the preceding section. Then she will have to determine whether it is reasonably
o As you know, before enumerating the five economic interests described at subsections (a) through (e), Section
87103 refers to financial effects on the oficial. The statute therefore envisions a possibility that conflicts of interest
can grow out of "personal financial effects," as well as from effects on the more narrowly described economic
interests listed at subsections (a) through (e). (Regulation 18703.5.) Such an effect is material if a decision would
foreseeably effect an official (or a member of the official's immediate family) in an amount of $250 or more in a
twelve month period. (Regulation 18705.5.)


File No. A-00-191
Page 6
foreseeable that the decision would have a material financial effect on her employer, presumably
by encouraging or discouraging Granite's purchase of supplies from Ferguson.
If there are circumstances suggesting that the decision would have a personal financial
effect on Councilmember Hodges, apart from the theoretical possibility inherent in any
transaction affecting her employer, then it might be appropriate to inquire into the foreseeable
effects on Councilmember Hodges, as well as looking into effects on her employer. Otherwise,
the analysis follows the same path outlined above in the discussion relating to Wessman.
For the sake of completeness we 4ote that, notwithstanding an apparent conflict of
interest, under certain limited circumstances a public official may still participate in a decision.
If the reasonably foreseeable effect of a decision on the official's economic interest is not
distinguishable from foreseeable effects on the public generally (Regulations 18700(7) and
18707), or if the officialis "legally required" to participate in the decision (within the meaning
of Regulations 18700(8) and 18708), participation in the decision would not violate the Act.
(Sections 87100, 87101, 87103.) Nothing in your request for advice suggests that either of these
exceptions would be applicable to Councilmember Hodges in decisions like those you describe,
but you should be aware of their existence.
If you have any other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel
By:

Senior Staff Counsel, Legal Division
